speaker: interviewee
text: May 13, 1996. Irmgard Blum Rowelsky survivor. Joyce Tapper, interviewer. San Bernardino, California, USA. English.

speaker: interviewer
text: May 13, 1996. The survivor is Irmgard Blum. My name is Joyce Tapper. And we are in San Bernardino, California. And in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Irmgard Blum, for short Irmy Blum. Everyone knows me by Irmy. I was born in Kaukehmen Kreis Niederung, East Prussia, not far from the Memel River. I-- we were a family of 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on the 23rd of April, 1913. And we had-- my mother had 13 children. Five of them died in birth. Eight of us were alive and lived in Kaukehmen. Kaukehmen was a small town of 3,000 inhabitants. We had a small synagogue. We couldn't call it really synagogue. There were only 12 Jewish families. And the Jewish and religious leader took care of all of us. His name was Mr. Anschel. And boys were particularly important, because they were trained for bar mitzvahs and for everything else, but the girls were not. So I insisted to go to Hebrew school. At that time already, I wanted to know more about my roots, my background. It was a lovely town. There was no anti-Semitism at that time. I went-- my brothers all went through school, and I and my other four went also to a grammar school and then to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say percentage of people were in that town? How many Jews to how many non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We only were 12 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right

speaker: interviewee
text: And the others were non-Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very small, may 10%.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. It's a very small percentage. We were treated well. We had all non-Jewish neighbors, who treated us with utmost respect. And therefore we were happy. As we went on in school, I felt that my brothers, who attended the high school, were very much harassed physically as well as mentally. My father had to go a number of times to the school to complain about their beatings that they performed on my brothers. When I came to graduate from my school, grammar school, I did not want to go to high school, because I did not want to be exposed to that kind of treatment. In grammar school, we found a little bit of harassment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any incident?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. I remember that our teacher would sometimes be a little short on us and would give us bad marks. And-- but not as bad. It was not as bad as my brothers experienced in high school. So when I graduated, I wanted to go to business college, out of Kaukehmen, to Tilsit. And I was accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Before you tell us about what Tilsit was like, describe a little more of your family life.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, we were-- the boys were all, after graduation, they had to be trained in business. And they all left-- these two boys. I had Paul and Esau, and they were trained in the business world in different cities. We had to pay the big companies for them to train my brothers. My sister Jenny, who also graduated from high school, became a legal secretary. And she left for Berlin to work as a paralegal secretary in Berlin. My sister, Dora, she was trained to be a seamstress, but she didn't like it. So she was trained to be a secretary. And she worked as a cashier and as a bookkeeper in a Jewish department store in Konigsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that your family was prosperous or comfortable? What word would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, because being that I was born during the First World War, it was very hard. When I was two years old, we moved into a larger apartment and which had a store. And my father would supply farmers, small and big farmers, with material and fur to make suits, coats lined with furs, and he would travel. We had a carriage and a horse. And he would travel to visit the farmers. And it was-- I wouldn't say prosperous, but we made a good living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you have plenty to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. It was not that easy, but being that my father worked with farmers, sometimes he got a little extra. And we all had to eat. Yes. And it was a-- it was really at the beginning, as far as I can remember, very nice. Life was entirely different. We had-- my father was-- and my mother were very traditional, not orthodox, but very traditional. We never worked on Saturday. We upheld all our holidays. We always went to Shul, what they're called. And we always went, and it was nice. And we celebrated bar mitzvah, had the whole family come to Kaukehmen, and we had a big spread for them. It was delightful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came from Tilsit-- also East Prussia-- they came from Tilsit and from Konigsberg and from Heidekrug, all over. And it was nice. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work in the business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother worked in the business that she did the bookkeeping. But she had enough to do with the eight children. We did have help. We had a woman come in on Sunday, the washing-- clothes washing-- that was a big procedure those days. She would come in and soak the laundry. The next day she would boil the laundry on a wooden stove. And the next day she would rinse it, and we had to go to a big farm that had lots of yards. And we would spread our laundry out there for it to be bleached by the sun. And then we had to get up early in the morning, 5:00 to do that, otherwise we wouldn't get a space to proceed with our laundry. And then at night, we would take it in, rinse it and dry it again on lines. And then the next day, we would go to a professional iron. It was a big company that had those automatic irons. And that took from Sunday to Wednesday. On Friday, she would come in to clean. But all of us had to work and to help. There was no getting away from our responsibilities. So-- and once we were finished, we could do what we wanted. I had a lot of friends-- girlfriends mostly-- and across our street, we had a big farm, a very big farm. And at wintertime, it was very much rain, and we had very severe winters, like in Siberia. We had lots of snow and lots of ice. And my greatest pleasure was, after I was finished with my obligation in my home, I went skating. And I had a wonderful time. So really it was a nice town, very happy town. The non-Jewish community was respectful as per se. And I-- we only noticed after we grew up that there was anti-Semitism brewing, and we felt it. That's why I didn't want to stay in Berlin to continue my education.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was accepted at [INAUDIBLE] Business College. I had to get up at 3:00 in the morning and bicycle about two hours to the river, where a boat took me to Tilsit. I got into Tilsit about 8:00, went to school, and got home at 6:00 at night. I did all my homework on the boat. And then that-- mostly I went to bed, because I had to get up that early. Then that was six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next six months, in wintertime, I stayed with my father's sister, who lived in Tilsit, and she had three boys. And she let me live with her. And I finished college about the third highest up. And I was very pleased, because some of the students from high school in San-- in Kaukehmen never made it. So that made me feel good. I worked hard. And then after that--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many years were you in this business school?

speaker: interviewee
text: One year. One year. After that, my uncle Morris, the brother of my father, invited me to stay some time with him in Wehlau. He was a very well-respected man in Wehlau. He belonged to the Lions at that time. And he had a shoe store. He had a lovely wife that came from Latvia, Mina, and his name was Morris Rowelsky, and he had two girls. And we have been close to that family ever since. After a while, my sister had left Kaukehmen-- the second one had left Kaukehmen for Konigsberg. That was also in East Prussia. And she worked as a secretary for a cousin of my father, [? Hoglund. ?] And she had a very, very good position. But she missed the family, so she asked me to join her and get a job in Konigsberg. Konigsberg was a very beautiful city, very beautiful. And here again, we did not feel anti-Semitism at all. But I could not get a job that I was trained for. So in the meantime, my sister Jenny, who lived now in Berlin and had a legal secretarial job with a very prominent attorney, encouraged me to come to Berlin. So I went to Berlin. And I-- with her we shared an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would this be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be-- it's hard to say. It would be about '32-- 1932. And we shared an apartment. I got a job with a lawyer. And he was the only lawyer, and I was the only employee. After about six months, he had to give up his practice, because he had a mental problem. He went to court and tore all the uniforms up. So he could not practice law anymore. So I had to give it up. It didn't take me long to get a job as a legal secretary for a divorce court lawyer. And she was a lawyer, and they had two other employees. I did not like that job, because in those days, to get a divorce was very difficult. You had to bring the worst out, sometimes exaggerate it, and sometimes just plain made up. And I felt if I continue, I will never ever marry. So I gave it up. It was difficult to find another job. So my father had a cousin, who was very well to do, in Berlin. And he asked me and so does his wife to come and join them and stay with them until I find a job. And they had two adorable ladies-- girls in my age. We had a marvelous time. And they had their old mother living with them. She was the dearest old lady. And somehow when the girls were a little bit tired of waiting on her, I took over. And she always said, Irmy, if I die, I will pray that you will get the best husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally I then found a job. And at a-- he was a Supreme Court lawyer, Doctor of Law and Doctor of Philosophy. And he worked for the brewery industry in the law department. He was a very, very fine [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] man that was most intellectual. And it was a pleasure to work for him. And he-- I worked there, and he had a small private-- What was his name and was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr.-- Dr. Hertzog. I unfortunately forgot his first name. He was a very handsome man. And he loved to take off in the afternoon and go dancing. And when he came back, he was so excited when the women thought he was a gigolo and wanted to pay him for dancing with him-- with them. So anyhow, he got married. We were invited to the wedding in a church. And on his honeymoon, he got killed. We don't know whether he was murdered or whether he committed suicide. On the day when he was supposed to go to the church, he came in our office, and we were three of us who worked for him. And he said I wish that they would never have come. And that was the last he ever talked to us. And then they came and investigated. But we never found out why he died. He had his honeymoon in Sicily, in Italy, and found-- and was found on the floor from apparently fell out from a window, and he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say he said they should never have come, who was the they do you think he--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- the day-- this day should never have come. In fact, we had a feeling that he was sorry that he was going to get married. That was our feeling. So after a while, we had to liquidate the office. And the office was closed. The second-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one-- one flight below us was a big brewery company. They called it the Brauer-Bund. And there were four executives, all lawyers, and all big people executives. And they offered me to come and work for them with a wage that was more than I was getting upstairs was Dr. Hertzog. They started me out as a statistician and secretary for ever-- whenever a lawyer needed me. I know their names. Dr. Schmidt was the top man. Dr. Dusen, Dr. Lubke, who I later found out after the war was president of Germany in Bonn, And Dr. Schwartz. They treated me with utmost respect. They knew that I was Jewish. And I was the only Jewish person there working. And every-- and then the-- we saw a dark cloud on the horizon when Hitler came in to Germany. And he came in to Berlin, and we did not like it at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: We, meaning your friends or--

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. My family were very concerned. I was concerned. The lower class of people loved him. The upper class of people felt that he is crazy, and he won't last long. So they did not take much notice of him. But I was very much concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the first thing that happened that was negative thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first thing what happened is he went to our president at time, the old senile von Hindenburg, Marshal von Hindenburg, and he proposed to him to have him let Hitler take over the regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: What I mean is, what was the first thing that you noticed in your life? Not a historical thing but what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I noticed was that he attracted too many people, promising them heaven on earth, promising them the best of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were concerned, but then what--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: --happened to you or what did you observe or see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very concerned, because I had my eyes wide open and my ears wide open. I watched, I read, and I saw. And when he went to Hindenburg, he asked, as I said before, to turn the regime over to him, under severe threats-- threats. He said that-- and promise and threats. So being that Hindenburg was already old, sick, and senile, he turned his government over to him. The first thing was that we experienced that he burned the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Reichstag. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He burned the Brandenburg Tor-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah-- where a lot of people lived-- not lived, I'm sorry-- worked there. And they-- and that is where the whole history of Germany from day one was stored. And he wanted to eliminate that. He didn't want any of it. So it burned. I think it was the-- the Reichstag, I'm sorry. The Reichstag. Takes me a little back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were you near the Reichstag? Did you see the fire? Were you near there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there. Yes. I was in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too far, but I was in Berlin, and I saw the flames. And we knew, and we were worried, because we knew that the Reichstag contained all the important documents that Germany had. That was destroyed, and that's exactly what Hitler wanted. Then unbeknown to the German public, and I will say this-- I didn't know either. I found out about it many years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to concentrate more on your own-- what happened to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --because this is very interesting, but I want to say, you know, you were working now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're living with your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the upper class ignored him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you? Let's not worry about the upper--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was still working there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every time Hitler spoke, we had to go in the meeting room and listen to Hitler with our arms up to say heil Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who made you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the executive board, all of them. They asked me to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how often did you have to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would not-- every time Hitler spoke officially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like every week, every day? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It could be once a week, it could be twice a week. I would not lift up my hand. And two colleagues of mine put their hands up so to show-- pretend-- that I put up my hand. The big boss, Dr. Schmidt, called me in. And he said-- at that time I was not married, and I had-- they called me [? Roshen. ?] My maiden name was Rowelsky. But they all called me [? Roshen. ?] [? Roshen. ?] You did not put up your hand. I said, Dr. Schmidt, would you put up your hand if you would know that they are going to kill you?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said no, I wouldn't. I said then why do you expect me to do it? He said I admire you that you don't give up your heritage for that man. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so he said, I tell you what, you'll never have to come in again. You are going to be excused. As we worked on, things became worse. And suddenly the government wanted to take over our business. And Hitler's best friend came into our office-- And who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to negotiate. Dr. Rohm. And I overheard a conversation with Dr. Rohm and Dr. Schmidt, telling him that he is concerned about Hitler. Mind you, he was a close friend of Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot tell exactly. It could be '34. Could be '34. I could not tell you exactly. And he said that he was very much concerned. Why? He said whenever he went in to visit his friend, Adolf, he couldn't sit. He wouldn't sit. He would pace up and down, up and down, never face him. It was most unusual for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally, he said I don't know what we can do. Later on, we found out that Dr. Rohm had planned to kill Hitler, to have a something, and all we found out that one high military official betrayed him with Hitler, and he was killed on the spot.
